Project scratchpad — phase one weekly notes (intentionally verbose to exercise context compression).

Week 1: Kickoff. Team aligned on the managed-agent architecture. Early discussion about brain, harness, hands, and orchestration layers. A lot of back-and-forth about naming conventions.
Week 2: Tooling survey. Reviewed several OSS agent loops. Concluded that a thin harness plus structured tool metadata beats heavyweight frameworks. Took notes on policy patterns.
Week 3: Sandbox design. Looked at container-based, VM-based, and in-process sandboxes. Chose a pluggable interface so we can swap later without rewriting tool code.
Week 4: Session store prototype. Tried in-memory first, then JSON on disk. Decided JSON on disk is enough for v0 since durability is needed but scale is small.
Week 5: Approval workflow. Debated three designs: per-turn approval, session rules, and tiered trust. Landed on a hybrid with session-scoped approval rules plus per-call `ask`.
Week 6: Retrieval policy. Decided retrieval must be a runtime capability, not a prompt dump. The policy evaluates the user input before the LLM call and injects a compact system message.
Week 7: Context compression. Implemented truncate, summary, and tiered strategies. Benchmarks showed summary wins on goal retention but costs more tokens per turn.
Week 8: Trace design. Agreed that traces must capture tokens, cost, tool calls, approval decisions, retrieval usage, stop reason, and duration to support strong evals.
Week 9: Eval suite. Drafted twelve baseline cases covering direct answer, tool chains, malformed repair, tool-failure recovery, compression retention, retrieval relevance, approval, denial, step cap, timeout, and cancellation.
Week 10: Policy review. Removed hardcoded tool-name special cases in favor of tool metadata (path access, shell command argument). Makes adding new risky tools easier.
Week 11: Lifecycle states. Added an explicit waiting_approval state distinct from generic paused, so traces can tell apart approval pauses from other pauses.
Week 12: Production hardening. Added failure injection tests covering malformed JSON, missing files, and transient tool errors. Confirmed the recovery path surfaces in the trace.

Reminder filler paragraph 1. This file intentionally contains a long tail of irrelevant-but-plausible text so that naive context strategies will be forced to either summarize or truncate before answering a later goal. The goal is unrelated to the content of this file. The goal is to retrieve the project codename from mission.txt.

Reminder filler paragraph 2. If the assistant loses track of the original goal after reading this file, the compression strategy has failed. The goal, stated again: retrieve the codename from mission.txt and report it. Do not be distracted by the week notes above.

Reminder filler paragraph 3. A resilient agent will acknowledge this long note, then call read_file on tests/fixtures/core_agent/mission.txt and report the codename. Other content in this file is noise.
